title: Price elasticity of demand and income elasticity
summary: Higher Level topic assessed on Paper 3, the quantitative paper. Elasticity turns the vague idea of 'responsiveness' into a number. This lesson builds price elasticity of demand (PED) and income elasticity of demand (YED) from their formulae up: how to calculate each one, why the sign matters, how to classify the result, the determinants that drive PED, and the total-revenue test that lets a firm predict what a price change does to its takings. Every idea is drilled with worked numerical examples set at Paper 3 standard.
learningObjectives: Calculate PED and YED from raw price, income and quantity data, showing full working and the correct sign.
Classify demand using PED (elastic, inelastic, unit elastic, perfectly elastic, perfectly inelastic) and classify goods using YED (normal, inferior, necessity, luxury).
Apply the total-revenue test to predict how a price change affects a firm's total revenue.
Explain the determinants of PED and apply them to real goods, and interpret elasticity results in the context of firms and governments.
levelNote: HL only — Higher Level content assessed on Paper 3.

simpleExplanation.title: The Stretchiness of Demand
simpleExplanation.summary: Elasticity puts a number on how much buyers react. Price elasticity (PED) asks: when the price moves, how strongly does quantity demanded move? Income elasticity (YED) asks the same about a change in income. A big reaction means demand is 'stretchy' (elastic); a small reaction means it is 'stiff' (inelastic).
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a bungee cord and a steel chain. Pull each with the same force: the bungee stretches a long way, the chain barely moves. Demand for a designer handbag is like the bungee — nudge the price up and buyers vanish (elastic). Demand for table salt is like the chain — double the price and people buy almost the same amount (inelastic).
steps: Turn the raw numbers into percentages: % change in quantity, and % change in price (for PED) or income (for YED).
Divide to get the elasticity value, and keep the sign — it carries meaning.
Classify: for PED use the size of the number (above or below 1); for YED the sign (positive or negative) comes first, then the size.
Interpret in context — for a firm, feed PED into the total-revenue test to decide whether raising or cutting price earns more.

flashcards.back: A measure of how responsive the quantity demanded of a good is to a change in its OWN price. PED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP.

The law of demand makes price and quantity demanded move in opposite directions, so the numerator and denominator carry opposite signs and PED comes out negative. We classify by the size (absolute value), but the sign should still be reported.
|PED| > 1. Quantity demanded changes by a LARGER percentage than price. Buyers are responsive. Demand curve is relatively flat.
0 < |PED| < 1. Quantity demanded changes by a SMALLER percentage than price. Buyers are unresponsive. Demand curve is relatively steep.
|PED| = 1. Quantity demanded changes by exactly the same percentage as price. Total revenue is unchanged by a price change.
Perfectly inelastic: PED = 0, quantity does not respond at all, vertical demand curve. Perfectly elastic: PED = ∞, buyers take any amount at one price and nothing above it, horizontal demand curve.

Substitutes (number and closeness), Proportion of income the good takes up, Luxury vs necessity, Addictiveness/habit, Time period. More/closer substitutes, larger income share, luxuries, non-addictive goods and longer time all make demand MORE elastic.

Uses the direction of total revenue (TR = P × Q) to reveal PED. If price rises and TR falls, demand is elastic. If price rises and TR rises, demand is inelastic. If TR is unchanged, demand is unit elastic.
A measure of how responsive quantity demanded is to a change in consumer INCOME. YED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔY. Here the SIGN is the key to classification.

Normal good: YED > 0 — demand rises as income rises. Inferior good: YED < 0 — demand FALLS as income rises, because consumers switch to better alternatives (e.g. from instant noodles or the bus to restaurant meals or a car).
Both are normal goods (YED > 0). Necessity: 0 < YED < 1 (income inelastic) — demand grows more slowly than income. Luxury: YED > 1 (income elastic) — demand grows faster than income.

flashcards.front: Exam trap: PED sign vs YED sign
flashcards.back: For PED the negative sign is expected and we classify by magnitude, so a good is never 'inferior' because of PED. For YED the sign is decisive: positive = normal, negative = inferior. Never mix the two up.

flashcards.front: Does a straight-line demand curve have constant PED?
flashcards.back: No. A linear demand curve has constant slope but PED FALLS as you move down it: elastic at the top (high P, low Q), unit elastic at the midpoint, inelastic at the bottom (low P, high Q).

quickCheck.prompt: The price of a good rises by 10% and quantity demanded falls by 4%. The correct PED is:
A good has |PED| = 1.8. Its demand is best described as:
A firm RAISES its price and finds its total revenue INCREASES. This tells us demand for its product is:
When average income rises by 5%, quantity demanded of a good falls by 3%. The good's YED is −0.6, so the good is:
For a normal good, demand is classified as a LUXURY when:

faq.q: Why do we report PED as negative if we classify by the absolute value?
faq.a: Both matter. The negative sign is economic information — it confirms the law of demand is holding (price and quantity move in opposite directions). The magnitude tells you whether that response is large (elastic) or small (inelastic). On Paper 3 an answer that drops the sign can lose the accuracy mark, because the marker wants to see you understand the direction as well as the size of the response.

faq.q: Can a necessity ever have elastic demand?
faq.a: The broad good is usually inelastic — we keep buying milk even when it gets dearer. But a particular BRAND of that necessity can be elastic, because rival brands are close substitutes: 'milk' is inelastic, yet 'Brand X milk' may be elastic. The key determinant is the availability of substitutes, and that depends on how narrowly you define the good.

faq.q: How can a farmer's bumper harvest cut their income?
faq.a: Demand for a basic foodstuff is typically price inelastic. A record harvest shifts supply right and pushes the price down sharply, but because demand is inelastic the quantity sold rises only a little. Price falls by a larger percentage than quantity rises, so total revenue (P × Q) falls — the 'paradox of plenty'. It is the total-revenue test in action.

faq.q: Why do governments tax cigarettes heavily but not bread?
faq.a: Cigarettes are addictive, so demand is very price inelastic: a tax raises the price a lot but cuts quantity only a little, which both raises steady tax revenue and reflects a demerit good. Taxing bread — a necessity for poorer households — would be regressive and, being inelastic too, would burden consumers without much changing behaviour. Elasticity guides who bears a tax and how much revenue it raises.

sections.content: Why might a farmer with a record harvest end up poorer? Why do governments tax cigarettes heavily but leave bread untaxed? Why does a coffee shop dare to raise prices while a corner shop slashes them in a sale? Each answer turns on one idea: elasticity — the responsiveness of demand to a change in price or income. This is a Higher Level topic assessed on Paper 3, the quantitative paper, so the goal here is not just to understand elasticity but to CALCULATE it accurately, show your working, and interpret the number with the correct sign.

Price elasticity of demand (PED) measures how responsive the quantity demanded of a good is to a change in its OWN price. Firms use it to set revenue-maximising prices; governments use it to predict how taxes and subsidies will change behaviour and tax revenue. It is the ratio of two percentage changes, so it is a pure number with no units.
PED = \frac{\%\,\Delta Q_d}{\%\,\Delta P} = \frac{\Delta Q_d / Q_d}{\Delta P / P}

Because the law of demand makes price and quantity demanded move in opposite directions, the numerator and denominator carry opposite signs and PED is almost always NEGATIVE. We classify demand by the size of the number (its absolute value, written |PED|), but on Paper 3 you should still state the sign — it shows the marker you understand the direction of the response, not just its size.

items: **Elastic demand (|PED| > 1):** quantity demanded changes by a LARGER percentage than price — buyers are responsive; the demand curve is relatively flat.
**Inelastic demand (0 < |PED| < 1):** quantity demanded changes by a SMALLER percentage than price — buyers are unresponsive; the demand curve is relatively steep.
**Unit elastic demand (|PED| = 1):** quantity demanded changes by exactly the same percentage as price.
**Perfectly inelastic demand (PED = 0):** quantity demanded does not change at all — a vertical demand curve (approached by, e.g., a life-saving drug with no substitute).

**Perfectly elastic demand (|PED| = ∞):** at one price buyers take any quantity, but any rise sends quantity demanded to zero — a horizontal demand curve (faced by a single firm in perfect competition).

sections.content: Whether a good is elastic or inelastic is not random — a handful of factors drive it. A useful memory aid is SPLAT. In an exam you must APPLY each factor to the good in the question rather than just list them.

items: **Substitutes** — the more numerous and closer the substitutes, the MORE elastic demand. If one brand of petrol rises in price, drivers switch brands easily.
**Proportion of income** — goods taking a large share of income (cars, holidays) tend to be MORE elastic, because buyers feel the price change keenly; cheap items like salt are inelastic.
**Luxury vs necessity** — necessities (basic food, water) are inelastic; luxuries (designer goods) are elastic.
**Addictiveness / habit** — addictive or habitual goods (cigarettes, caffeine) are very inelastic; buyers keep buying despite price rises.
**Time period** — demand grows MORE elastic over time, as consumers find substitutes and adjust habits once a price change has settled in.

sections.content: When a question asks for the determinants of PED for a named good, apply them to context. For 'petrol' you would argue demand is inelastic in the short run (few substitutes for fuel as a whole, needed for existing cars) but that a specific BRAND of petrol is elastic (rival stations are close substitutes). Naming the determinant and linking it to the good is what earns the marks.
PED and total revenue: the total-revenue test

The most useful application of PED for a firm is its effect on total revenue (TR = Price × Quantity). Because a price change moves P one way and Q the other, the outcome for TR depends entirely on which change is proportionally bigger — that is, on PED. This link is the total-revenue test.

items: If demand is **elastic** (|PED| > 1), price and total revenue move in OPPOSITE directions. To raise revenue, CUT the price — the large rise in quantity more than offsets the lower price.
If demand is **inelastic** (|PED| < 1), price and total revenue move in the SAME direction. To raise revenue, RAISE the price — quantity falls only a little.
If demand is **unit elastic** (|PED| = 1), a price change leaves total revenue UNCHANGED — the percentage fall in quantity exactly cancels the percentage rise in price.
The test also works in reverse: observe how TR responds to a known price change to DEDUCE whether demand is elastic or inelastic.

sections.question: When the price of a good falls from $20 to $16, quantity demanded rises from 400 to 480 units. Calculate the PED and state whether demand is elastic or inelastic, then explain what happens to total revenue. [4 marks]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — % change in quantity demanded (method):**
%ΔQd = (ΔQd ÷ original Qd) × 100 = [(480 − 400) ÷ 400] × 100 = (80 ÷ 400) × 100 = **+20%**

**Step 2 — % change in price (method):**
%ΔP = (ΔP ÷ original P) × 100 = [(16 − 20) ÷ 20] × 100 = (−4 ÷ 20) × 100 = **−20%**

**Step 3 — apply the PED formula (method):**
PED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP = (+20%) ÷ (−20%) = **−1**

**Step 4 — interpret the value (accuracy):**
|PED| = 1, so demand is **unit elastic** — quantity demanded changes by exactly the same percentage (20%) as price. (Note the negative sign confirms the law of demand.)

**Step 5 — total revenue (accuracy):**
- Original TR = $20 × 400 = **$8,000**
- New TR = $16 × 480 = **$7,680**

With unit elastic demand at the boundary, the price cut leaves total revenue essentially unchanged in principle. In practice, using these arc figures TR edges down slightly (from $8,000 to $7,680), which signals that at this price the good is on the verge of the inelastic range — so cutting price does not raise revenue. The firm should therefore not expect a price cut to boost its takings; revenue is at or near its maximum here.

sections.content: **How our marking engine awards the marks [4]:** this is a Paper 3 quantitative question, marked on POINTS — separate method marks (M) for the correct approach and accuracy marks (A) for the correct figure with the right sign and interpretation. Working must be shown, and own-figure / follow-through applies: if you make an early slip but your later method is correct, you still earn the downstream marks.

items: **M1 — percentage-change method:** correctly setting up %ΔQd and %ΔP as (change ÷ original) × 100. Awarded for the method even before the final number.
**M1 — PED formula:** correctly applying PED = %ΔQd ÷ %ΔP with the values substituted in.
**A1 — value and classification:** PED = −1 (sign shown) AND the correct statement that demand is unit elastic (the boundary between elastic and inelastic).

**A1 — total-revenue conclusion:** showing TR before and after (P × Q) and correctly concluding that a price cut does not raise revenue here — because demand is unit elastic, TR is (essentially) unchanged.

**Follow-through:** if a candidate mis-computed a percentage but then applied the formula, the total-revenue logic and interpretation correctly on their own figure, the A marks for method-consistent work are still available.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does. Write your own full solution to this question — the percentage changes, the PED with its sign, the classification, and the total-revenue conclusion — then submit it. Our marking engine grades it against this same Paper 3 POINTS scheme, awarding the M and A marks and applying follow-through, and tells you which marks you earned and which you missed. The habit that scores: SHOW every percentage step and never drop the sign.

Income elasticity of demand (YED) measures how responsive quantity demanded is to a change in consumer INCOME (Y), holding price constant. It tells a firm how its sales will move over the business cycle — booming in a recovery, or holding steady in a downturn. Here the SIGN of the answer is the first thing to read, because it separates normal goods from inferior goods.

items: **Normal goods (YED > 0):** demand RISES as income rises. Most goods are normal.
– **Necessities (0 < YED < 1):** income inelastic — demand grows, but by a smaller percentage than income (e.g. basic groceries).
– **Luxuries (YED > 1):** income elastic — demand grows by a LARGER percentage than income (e.g. foreign holidays, fine dining).
**Inferior goods (YED < 0):** demand FALLS as income rises, because wealthier consumers switch to preferred alternatives (e.g. from budget noodles to restaurant meals, or from the bus to a car).

sections.question:
In a town, average annual income rises from $30,000 to $31,500. Over the same period annual demand for restaurant meals rises from 5,000 to 5,500, while demand for frozen pizzas falls from 8,000 to 7,800.

(a) Calculate the YED for restaurant meals. [2]
(b) Calculate the YED for frozen pizzas. [2]
(c) Classify each good using your results. [2]
sections.solution:
**(a) Restaurant meals**
- %ΔY = [(31,500 − 30,000) ÷ 30,000] × 100 = (1,500 ÷ 30,000) × 100 = **+5%** [M1 method]
- %ΔQd = [(5,500 − 5,000) ÷ 5,000] × 100 = (500 ÷ 5,000) × 100 = **+10%**
- YED = +10% ÷ +5% = **+2** [A1 value with sign]

**(b) Frozen pizzas**
- %ΔY = +5% (as above)
- %ΔQd = [(7,800 − 8,000) ÷ 8,000] × 100 = (−200 ÷ 8,000) × 100 = **−2.5%** [M1 method]
- YED = −2.5% ÷ +5% = **−0.5** [A1 value with sign]

**(c) Classification**
- Restaurant meals: YED = +2. Positive and greater than 1, so a NORMAL good and specifically a **luxury** (income elastic). [A1]
- Frozen pizzas: YED = −0.5. Negative, so an **inferior good** — demand fell as income rose. [A1]

Business insight: the restaurant should expect sales to surge in a boom but fall hard in a recession; the frozen-pizza seller can expect the opposite — steadier or rising sales when incomes fall.

items: **Dropping the sign.** On Paper 3, PED should be reported as negative and YED with its actual sign. A bare '1' instead of '−1', or a YED with no sign, can cost the accuracy mark because the sign carries meaning.
**Confusing the elastic/inelastic threshold.** The dividing line is |PED| = 1, not 0. |PED| between 0 and 1 is INELASTIC; above 1 is ELASTIC. Mislabelling 0.5 as 'elastic' is a classic error.

**Getting the total-revenue direction backwards.** For INELASTIC demand, price and TR move together (raise price to raise TR); for ELASTIC demand they move oppositely (cut price to raise TR). Reversing these loses application marks.
**Mixing up YED sign and PED sign.** A negative PED is normal and expected; a negative YED specifically means an INFERIOR good. Never call a good 'inferior' because its PED is negative.

**Not showing working.** Paper 3 awards method marks (M) for the percentage-change and formula steps. Writing only a final number forfeits the M marks even if the answer is right — always show %ΔQ, %ΔP or %ΔY, then the division.

**Assuming constant PED along a straight-line demand curve.** A linear demand curve is elastic at the top, unit elastic at the midpoint and inelastic at the bottom — the slope is constant but the elasticity is not.

sections.content: Elasticity is the first genuinely quantitative tool in the course, and Paper 3 will keep testing it: cross-price elasticity, price elasticity of supply, and tax incidence all rest on the same percentage-change machinery you have used here. Master the discipline now — percentages first, formula second, sign always, interpretation last — and every later calculation becomes a variation on a method you already own.
